disclaimer: Educational assessment. CARSI is an IICRC-approved CEC provider; this is CARSI-original Australian training, not IICRC certification.
note: Australian-produced. Grounded in the course's own modules. Founder to review before wiring live.

quizzes.title: Introduction to Infrared Thermography for Drying — Knowledge Check

questions.questionText: On a thermogram of a wet building material, what does a cooler surface temperature typically indicate?
options: The surface has fully dried and needs no further attention
The camera's emissivity setting is too high
Higher moisture content remaining within the material's depth
The material is non-porous and cannot hold water
questions.sourceRef: M1 — Thermal Energy Concepts: Surface Temperature Dynamics

questions.questionText: Which three principles does the course identify as governing how thermal energy moves through materials and space?
options: Conduction, convection and radiation
Evaporation, condensation and sublimation
Absorption, reflection and transmission
Wicking, capillarity and diffusion
questions.sourceRef: M1 — Thermal Energy Concepts: Heat Transfer Principles

questions.questionText: What distinguishes a quantitative infrared camera output from a qualitative one?
options: It only displays a generic greyscale heat approximation
It provides calibrated exact temperature readings usable as quantifiable metrics for trending drying progress
It removes the need for any analysis software
It works only with shortwave infrared bands
questions.sourceRef: M2 — Infrared Camera Technologies: Qualitative vs Quantitative Outputs

questions.questionText: According to the course, what does higher detector pixel density (sensor resolution) in a camera primarily assist with?
options: Reducing the camera's tolerance to water and dust intrusion
Removing the need to match infrared bands to the material scanned
Lowering the purchase price of the camera
Identifying minute moisture across wall cavities and intricate textures
questions.sourceRef: M2 — Infrared Camera Technologies: Sensor Resolution Factors

questions.questionText: How does thermography help locate hidden leak sources behind or beneath structures?
options: Through differential temperature analysis, denoting areas cooler than expected
By measuring the surface hardness of building materials
By reading the emissivity rating of dry insulation
By capturing high-resolution visible-light photographs of pipe runs
questions.sourceRef: M3 — Moisture Detection Applications: Locating Hidden Leak Sources

questions.questionText: How is even heat-drying across an enclosed restorative chamber best verified over time?
options: By taking a single thermogram only at the end of the job
By measuring outdoor air temperature alone
By comparing initial thermograms against progress benchmarks, supported by periodic moisture-meter checks
By relying solely on the camera's greyscale image without any other readings
questions.sourceRef: M3 — Moisture Detection Applications: Verifying Heat Drying Uniformity

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a stated limitation of moisture-detection cameras?
options: They can only be operated outdoors in direct sunlight
Contact barriers such as vinyl wallpaper impede moisture evaporation and dense materials attenuate heat, limiting insight into interstitial layers
They read temperatures accurately through any material regardless of density
They completely replace the need for moisture meters and hygrometers
questions.sourceRef: M4 — Safely Applying Thermography: Understanding Limitations

questions.questionText: Which complementary methods does the course recommend to confirm infrared moisture findings before demolition or equipment decisions?
options: X-ray scanners and ultrasound imaging
Visible-light cameras used on their own
Emissivity calibration cards used in isolation
Moisture meters, hygrometers and tracer dyes
questions.sourceRef: M5 — Result Analysis and Applications: Complementary Techniques
